content.text: When Anthropic sends your data to a third-party infrastructure provider — AWS for compute, a monitoring vendor, or a safety evaluation partner — that third party becomes a

. Under GDPR Article 28 and equivalent privacy regulations, you are responsible for ensuring that every subprocessor in the chain provides equivalent data protection guarantees. This is not a one-time check at contract signing; it is an ongoing process triggered every time Anthropic adds or changes a subprocessor.

Your DPA with Anthropic is only as strong as the weakest link in Anthropic's subprocessor chain. Understanding that chain — and having a process to review changes to it — is a core enterprise procurement responsibility.
A subprocessor is any third party that a processor (Anthropic) engages to process personal data on the controller's (your) behalf. For Claude API use cases, the primary subprocessors are:
Request metadata, error logs, latency data
Subset of flagged requests for safety review
Anthropic publishes its current subprocessor list at

(consult the current page; the list changes as infrastructure evolves). Your DPA should require Anthropic to notify you ≥30 days before adding or replacing any subprocessor that processes Customer Data.

attrs.alt: Subprocessor review and approval workflow with objection paths

content.text: The subprocessor review process has five stages, each with defined owners and time constraints:

When Anthropic notifies you of a subprocessor change, your Privacy team (or designated DPA owner) must: log the notification immediately with a timestamp, identify whether the new subprocessor processes Customer Data or only internal operational data, and assign a reviewer based on data classification.

Subprocessor Notification Log Entry:
  Date received: YYYY-MM-DD
  Notified by: Anthropic privacy notification service
  Subprocessor name: [SP Name]
  Effective date: YYYY-MM-DD (≥30 days from notification)
  Change type: ADD | REPLACE | REMOVE
  Customer Data processed: YES | NO | UNCLEAR
  Assigned reviewer: [Name, Team]
  Review deadline: [Effective date - 15 days]
Determine what tier of data the new subprocessor will access. This classification drives the review depth required:
Notification only — no detailed review needed
Request metadata without content, user IDs
Prompt content, response content, business data
PHI, PII under GDPR, financial data, state secrets
Full review + legal sign-off + DPA amendment if needed
The security review validates that the subprocessor meets your security standards. Required artifacts for Tier 2+ subprocessors:

— less than 12 months old. Verify the report covers the relevant trust service criteria (Security is minimum; Availability and Confidentiality are preferred). Check that the period covered overlaps with the proposed go-live date.
— if the subprocessor is not SOC 2 certified, an in-scope ISO 27001 certificate is acceptable for EU-based entities.
— where will data at rest be stored? Where will processing occur? Is this compatible with your data residency requirements?
— for Tier 3 data, a summary of the most recent pen test and remediation status.
— what specific Customer Data does this subprocessor access? Under what circumstances? For what purposes?
The approval decision is binary: accept or object. The decision must be documented with rationale regardless of outcome.

Subprocessor Review Decision Record:
  SP Name: [Name]
  Review date: YYYY-MM-DD
  Reviewer(s): [Names + roles]
  Decision: APPROVED | OBJECTED

  If APPROVED:
    Basis: [e.g., SOC 2 Type II dated YYYY-MM; US jurisdiction
            consistent with existing data residency posture]
    Conditions: [any conditions, e.g., "approved for Tier 1-2 data only"]
    Next review trigger: [annual review OR next SP change notification]

  If OBJECTED:
    Basis: [specific risk: jurisdiction incompatibility, no SOC 2,
            data access scope exceeds what is necessary]
    Mitigation options considered:
      1. Route data around SP: [feasible / not feasible]
      2. Negotiate exception: [proposed approach]
      3. Exit service: [threshold / timeline]
    Written objection sent to Anthropic: YYYY-MM-DD
Approved subprocessors require ongoing monitoring, not just point-in-time review:
Annual re-review: pull updated SOC 2 / ISO certificate; verify no material changes to data access scope
Incident monitoring: subscribe to the subprocessor's security status page; flag any incidents that could affect Customer Data

Re-review trigger: any change in your own data classification (e.g., you start sending PHI to Claude when you previously sent only non-regulated data) requires re-reviewing all existing subprocessors against the new data tier
DPA amendment tracking: if a new subprocessor requires a DPA amendment (e.g., new SCCs for a new transfer mechanism), track the amendment signature timeline separately from the technical approval
Every organization processing personal data through Claude should maintain a subprocessor register — a living document that maps each of Anthropic's subprocessors to your own compliance posture:

# Anthropic Subprocessor Register
# Owner: Privacy Team | Updated: Quarterly

| Subprocessor | Role | Data Tier Accessed | Jurisdiction | SOC 2 / ISO | Status | Last Reviewed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AWS (us-east-1) | Inference compute & storage | Tier 2 | US | SOC 2 Type II (2024-09) | Approved | 2024-11-15 |
| AWS (eu-west-1) | EU data residency inference | Tier 2 | EU | SOC 2 Type II (2024-09) | Approved | 2024-11-15 |
| [Monitoring vendor] | Metrics & logs | Tier 1 | US | SOC 2 Type II (2024-06) | Approved | 2024-08-01 |
| [Safety eval partner] | Flagged content review | Tier 2 | US | SOC 2 Type II (2024-03) | Approved, conditional | 2024-09-15 |

## Conditional Approvals
- [Safety eval partner]: Approved for safety review only; Customer Data access limited to
  flagged prompts per Anthropic's Trust & Safety process. Re-review required if scope expands.

## Pending Reviews
- None

## Historical Objections
- YYYY-MM: [SP Name] — objected due to [reason]. Mitigated by [action].
The Objection Process — Step by Step
If your review concludes that a subprocessor is not acceptable, your DPA clause gives you the right to formally object. The objection process:

Send written objection to Anthropic's privacy/legal contact within 15 days of notification. The objection must state the specific basis (jurisdiction incompatibility, lack of security certification, excessive data access scope).

Anthropic has 10 days to respond with either: (a) a proposed mitigation (e.g., configuring the subprocessor to not access your specific tenant's data, or providing a contractual carve-out); or (b) confirmation that it cannot accommodate your objection.
If Anthropic proposes a mitigation: assess whether the mitigation actually addresses your compliance risk. Engage legal to review any proposed contract amendments.

If Anthropic cannot accommodate your objection: you have the right to terminate the affected services with 30 days' notice without penalty (per the DPA clause you negotiated). This is the exit provision that makes your objection right meaningful.
Document every step with timestamps. If you are GDPR-regulated, this documentation may be required for DPA records or supervisory authority inquiries.
An objection right without a termination provision is decorative. Before signing the DPA, confirm that your objection clause includes a penalty-free exit option.
Two-Sided Subprocessor Risk: Your Own Chain
The subprocessor review focuses on Anthropic's supply chain. But you are also a processor for
customers — and your Claude-powered features make Anthropic's subprocessors
in your chain. This has practical implications:
Your customer DPAs must disclose Anthropic (and Anthropic's subprocessors).
If your customers require a list of your subprocessors, Anthropic and its cloud provider must appear on it.
Your customer notification obligations run in parallel.
When Anthropic notifies you of a new subprocessor, you may be obligated to notify your own customers within the same 30-day window — before the subprocessor change takes effect.
Your customers' objection rights flow through to Anthropic.
If a customer objects to a subprocessor in your chain that turns out to be Anthropic's AWS region, you need to have mapped this dependency in advance to respond intelligently.

Subprocessor Cascade — What to Disclose to Your Customers:

Your Company (Controller / Processor)
  → Anthropic (Subprocessor)
      → AWS us-east-1 (Sub-subprocessor)
      → [Monitoring vendor] (Sub-subprocessor)
      → [Safety eval partner] (Sub-subprocessor, conditional)

Your customer DPA subprocessor list must include at minimum:
  - Anthropic, Inc. — AI model inference (US)
  - Amazon Web Services — Cloud infrastructure (US or EU depending on routing)

If your DPA with your customer uses a 'specific list' model rather than
a 'category' model, you may need to list all of Anthropic's subprocessors.
If it uses a category model ('cloud infrastructure providers in US/EU'),
Anthropic's chain may be covered without explicit enumeration.
SP change takes effect without your review
Subscribe to Anthropic's sub-processor notification service in the DPA; verify the subscription is active annually
Deemed acceptance in many DPA frameworks
Log notification day 0; set 10-day review deadline in team calendar; auto-escalate if no decision
Tier 0/1 auto-approval applied to Tier 2 data
Reduced review rigor for data that needs full security check
Classification step (Stage 2) must be completed before applying auto-approval shortcut
Negotiate termination-for-SP-objection clause before signing DPA
Register not updated to reflect your customers' scope
Annual refresh of your customer-facing SP list to match Anthropic's current SP list

Manual monitoring of Anthropic's subprocessor notification channel is error-prone. A missed notification means a subprocessor change goes live without your review — a compliance gap. Automate the intake pipeline:

via cron or a CI pipeline. A weekly cadence ensures you detect changes well within the 30-day notification window even if Anthropic's email notification is missed. Store the snapshot in version control so the change history is auditable.
For Tier 2 and Tier 3 subprocessors, standardize the security review using a scorecard rather than free-form assessment. A scorecard produces consistent, auditable, and comparable decisions:
Access limited to stated purpose = 5, Broad access = 2, Unrestricted = 0

Weighted scores: 4.0–5.0 = auto-approve, 3.0–3.9 = approve with conditions, 2.0–2.9 = object and negotiate, < 2.0 = object and prepare for exit. The numeric threshold removes ambiguity from the approval decision and creates a clear audit trail.
Integrating Subprocessor Reviews into Your Vendor Management Program

Subprocessor reviews do not exist in isolation — they are one input into a broader vendor risk management program. Integrate the Claude subprocessor process with your existing vendor management framework:
Use the same subprocessor register format for all your AI vendors, not just Anthropic. Consistency makes cross-vendor comparison and audit responses faster.
Define in advance which risk committee receives escalations for objected subprocessors. An objection that reaches the Privacy team with no clear escalation path often stalls.
Include Anthropic's subprocessor list validation in your annual AI vendor review, alongside the DPA renewal check, SOC 2 report review, and incident log review.
If your Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for Claude-based processing lists specific subprocessors, any subprocessor change may trigger a DPIA update — which has its own governance timeline.
If you are obligated to notify your own customers when your subprocessors change, coordinate the Anthropic notification receipt with your customer notification workflow to avoid timeline violations.
Use this checklist as a starting point for each Tier 2+ subprocessor review. Customize to your compliance requirements:

## Subprocessor Review Checklist — Tier 2 / Tier 3

### Receipt & Classification
- [ ] Notification logged with receipt date and effective date
- [ ] Data tier classification completed
- [ ] Reviewer assigned; deadline set (effective date - 15 days)

### Security Documents (request from Anthropic if not in notification)
- [ ] SOC 2 Type II report (current year): obtained / dated: ________
- [ ] ISO 27001 certificate (if EU SP): obtained / expires: ________
- [ ] Pen test summary (Tier 3 only): obtained / dated: ________
- [ ] Data flow description: reviewed

### Jurisdiction Analysis
- [ ] Where is data at rest? ________
- [ ] Where does processing occur? ________
- [ ] Compatible with our data residency requirements? YES / NO / CONDITIONAL
- [ ] SCCs or IDTA required? YES (arrange) / NO

### Data Access Scope
- [ ] What specific Customer Data does this SP access?
- [ ] Is the scope limited to stated purpose? YES / NO / UNCLEAR
- [ ] Does scope exceed what our DPA with Anthropic permits? YES (object) / NO

### Decision
- [ ] Scorecard score: ___ / 5.0
- [ ] Decision: APPROVE / APPROVE WITH CONDITIONS / OBJECT
- [ ] Conditions (if any): ________
- [ ] Written objection sent (if applicable): date ________
- [ ] Register updated: date ________
- [ ] Affected engineering teams notified: date ________
- [ ] Customer notification triggered (if required): date ________

The subprocessor review process is a five-stage workflow triggered by vendor notifications: receive and log the notification, classify data tier, conduct a security review appropriate to the tier, make and document the approval decision, and maintain ongoing governance. Build a subprocessor register that maps Anthropic's subprocessors to your compliance posture, updated quarterly. Exercise your objection right — backed by a penalty-free termination clause — when a subprocessor change introduces unacceptable risk. Remember that Anthropic's subprocessors become sub-subprocessors in your chain, requiring disclosure in your own customer DPAs. The register, the review process, and the objection mechanism are only valuable if the notification subscription is active and the review window is monitored — an expired review window without a decision is a gap in your compliance posture, not an automatic approval.
